Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-144 
Thank you for your letter requesting aaVlce regarding 
possible conflicts of interest between your professional 
activities and your position as a member of the California 
PUblic Broadcasting Commlssion (CPBC). 
You are a professional 
journalist; you write for several publications, and regularly 
appear on commercial televislon. 
In aaaltion, you own a weekly 
newspaper. 
The newspaper sells advertising space, and, as a 
siaeline, also performs commercial graphics work for a variety 
of customers. 
The Political Reform Act requires a public official to 
disqualify hlmself from making or participating in any 
governmental decision in which he has a financial interest. 
Government Code Section 87100.!/ 
It does not limit the 
nongovernmental activitles of a pUblic official in any way.~/ 
Therefore, the Poli tical Reform Act does not prevent you from 
engaging in any of the professional activities described in your 
letter. 
However, because it is possible tnat you may be 
required to disqualify yourself from certain decisions of the 
CPEe as a resulc of these activities, I will discuss the 
!/ All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise stated. 
~/ There are some other laws, notably Section 1090, which 
contain additional prohibitions on tne activltles ot public 
officials. 
You may cheCK with your agency counselor the 
Atcorney General for advice on tnese provisions. 


Larry Remer 
June 23, 1983 
Page 2 
disqualification provisions of the Act briefly as they apply to 
you.ll 
Disqualificationil is required under Section 87100 as to 
any ePBC decision in which you have a "financial interest." 
A 
financial interest is defined in Section 87103: 
An official nas a financlal interest in a decision 
witnin the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable 
thd~ tne decision will nave a material 
financlal effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the pUblic generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect lnvestment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institu~ion in the regular course of 
business on terms availaole to the public wlthout 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
recelved by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in wnich the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holdS any position of management. 
Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Political Reform Act may 
require you to disqualify yourself from CPBC decisions affecting 
your newspaper, affecting public broadcasting stations which 
nave been sources of income to you in the past 12 months, or 
affecting your own personal income. 
II 
I have also enclosed a Commission pamphlet on 
conflicts of interest for your information. 
il When you are required to disqualify yourself from a 
decision you may not vote on, particlpate in, or use your 
official position to influence the decision. 
Section 87100; 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (copy enclosed) . 


Larry Remer 
June 23, 19b3 
Page 3 
Decisions Affecting Your Newspaper 
Although you aid not state the value of your investment in 
your newspaper, I assume it exceeds $1,000. 
Therefore, under 
Section 87l03(a), you have a financial 1nterest in a CPBC 
deC1sion if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will 
have a material financial effect on your newspaper. 
For 
example, if a CPBC decision would have a materia121 financial 
effect on your newspaper's income from paia advertising, 
disqualification would be required. 
Decisions Affecting Sources of Income 
From your letter, it appears that you receive income as a 
journalist from pUblic oroadcasting stations. 
If you have 
received $250 or more from anyone station in the past 12 
months, that stat10n is a "source of income" to you. 
If it is 
reasonably foreseeable that any CPBC decision will have a 
material~7 financial effect on such a station, you must 
disqual1fy yourself from thac decision. 
Even if you do not receive income directly from a public 
oroadcasting station, tne station may oe consiaered a source of 
lncome to you if it nas paid your newspaper for advertising or 
other services in tne past year. 
If you own 10% or more of the 
newspaper, a pro rata share of all gross income to the newspaper 
is income to you.V-Section 82030(a). 
If your l2E.£ rata share 
of the gross income to your newspaper over the past 12 months 
from a public broadcasting station has been $250 or more, that 
station is a source of income to you. 
If it is reasonaoly 
foreseeable that a CPBC decision will have a material financial 
effect on such a oroadcasting station, you must disqualify 
yourself from the decision. 
21 
Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (1) defines when an effect on a business entity such as 
a newspaper is "material." 
(Copy enclosed.) 
§/ 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (b) (1) . 
21 
If you own 100% of the newspaper, all income received 
by the newspaper 1S treated as if it nad Deen paid directly to 
you. 


Larry Remer 
June j.j, 19d3 
Page 4 
Decisions Affecting Your Personal Income 
If it is reasonaoly foreseeable that a CPBC decislon would 
have a direct and material financial effect on your personal 
income~/ as a journalist, you must, of course, disqualify 
yourself trom that decision. 
I nope that this letter has been helpful. 
If you have a 
question about whetner you may particlpate in any particular 
CPBC decision, do not hesitate to ask us for further advice. 
BAl"!: pIn 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours,

sections.conclusion: Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Political Reform Act may require you to disqualify yourself from CPBC decisions affecting your newspaper, affecting public broadcasting stations which have been sources of income to you in the past 12 months, or affecting your own personal income. The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from engaging in any of the professional activities described in your letter. However, you must disqualify yourself from decisions in which you have a financial interest as defined in Section 87103.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Larry Remer's professional journalism activities, including ownership of a newspaper and receipt of income from public broadcasting stations, create conflicts of interest that would prevent him from serving on the California Public Broadcasting Commission?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit Remer's professional activities, but he must disqualify himself from CPBC decisions affecting his newspaper, public broadcasting stations that are sources of income to him, or his personal income, as defined under Section 87103.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is clear FPPC advice letter with well-defined question and conclusion sections. OCR quality is good with only minor typos. Letter provides detailed guidance on disqualification provisions applicable to the requestor's specific circumstances. Includes statutory references and regulatory citations.

QUESTION: about whetner you may particlpate in any particular 
CPBC decision, do not hesitate to ask us for further advice. 
BAl"!: pIn 
Enclosure

CONCLUSION: Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Political Reform Act may require you to disqualify yourself from CPBC decisions affecting your newspaper, affecting public broadcasting stations which have been sources of income to you in the past 12 months, or affecting your own personal income. The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from engaging in any of the professional activities described in your letter. However, you must disqualify yourself from decisions in which you have a financial interest as defined in Section 87103.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-144 addresses conflicts of interest for Larry Remer, a journalist and newspaper owner serving on the California Public Broadcasting Commission. The FPPC concludes that while the Political Reform Act does not prohibit his professional activities, he must disqualify himself from specific CPBC decisions where he has a defined financial interest under Government Code Section 87103.
